headline: The Grand and The Glen open in Toledo — Pivotal Housing Partners delivers 70 affordable units, nearly $30M investment across two neighborhoods
paragraphs: The City of Toledo joined Pivotal Housing Partners, community leaders, and residents for ribbon-cutting ceremonies on July 8, 2026 marking the completion of two affordable workforce housing developments — The Grand and The Glen — representing nearly $30 million in total investment. The Glen, at 3440 Glendale Ave. in south Toledo, occupies a 3.66-acre portion of the former Southland Shopping Center site and delivers 50 one- and two-bedroom apartments with rents starting under $800 per month. The Grand, sited at the corner of North Detroit and Grand avenues in central Toledo on the former Driggs Dairy property, consists of 20 three- and four-bedroom townhomes with rents starting at just over $1,000 per month. Both communities serve households earning between 50% and 70% of area median income (AMI) and together generated over 250 construction jobs during a February 2025–July 2026 build cycle.

Both developments were financed through a combination of Low-Income Housing Tax Credit equity allocated by the Ohio Housing Finance Agency (OHFA) and a KeyBank Community Development construction loan totaling $15.5 million for the two-site project. Total financing reached approximately $36.4 million across the development package. Both communities offer on-site management, fitness centers, playgrounds, pavilions, computer and printer stations, and community gathering spaces. The Southland adaptive-reuse model at The Glen — converting a long-underutilized retail anchor into residential density — is the approach that Toledo's Land Bank and Community Improvement Corporation have been seeking to replicate at comparable commercial corridors across the city.
why_it_matters: Workforce housing at the 50–70% AMI band is one of the most persistent gaps in Toledo's housing pipeline — units priced too high for deep-subsidy programs and too modest for market-rate developers. The Grand and The Glen address this gap in two neighborhoods — central Toledo and south Toledo — that have limited proximity to the riverfront catalysts driving downtown investment. The Southland repurposing at The Glen is particularly notable: it converts a dead retail anchor into housing density without raising taxes, the same model the city has been advancing through the TCIC shovel-ready pipeline. At nearly $30M in investment and 70 units, this is among the largest affordable housing completions in Toledo in recent years, and a direct proof point for the Welcome Home Ohio and OHFA tax credit pipeline Toledo leaders have been cultivating.
